---
title: "Read-only versus Writable R Objects"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Read-only versus Writable R Objects}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

# Motivation

@vaughan already provides some details. This vignette expands on the differences between `cpp4r` and `Rcpp`.

# Read and write

In `cpp4r`, when you declare a variable, it is read-only by default. This is different from `Rcpp`, where variables are read-write by default.

The following code will not compile in `cpp4r`:

```cpp
[[cpp4r::register]] integers square_coordinates_(integers x) {
  integers out = x;
  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); ++i) {
    out[i] = x[i] * x[i];
  }
  return out;
}
```

The error message is:

```bash
code.cpp:515:10: error: lvalue required as left operand of assignment
  515 |     out[i] = x[i] * x[i];
```

To fix this, you need to explicitly declare the variable as writable:

```cpp
[[cpp4r::register]] integers square_coordinates_(integers x) {
  writable::integers out = x;
  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); ++i) {
    out[i] = x[i] * x[i];
  }
  return out;
}
```

The arguments are also read-only by default in `cpp4r`, unless you declare them as writable, as in the following example:

```cpp
[[cpp4r::register]] integers square_coordinates2_(writable::integers x) {
  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); ++i) {
    x[i] = x[i] * x[i];
  }
  return x;
}
```

# References
